name: Arnold Hill
short_description: A modest rockhounding site in the Adirondack foothills yielding garnet, mica, and quartz specimens. Public access on mixed terrain near AuSable.
description:
Arnold Hill sits within the metamorphic bedrock of the Adirondack Mountains, a region with rich mineral diversity from ancient continental collisions. This location offers collectors the chance to find almandine garnets (typically dark red to brown), muscovite mica sheets, and quartz crystals weathered from the local metamorphic host rock. The site is accessible to rockhounds of varying skill levels, though terrain is uneven and overgrown in places. Success here depends on understanding the local geology - Precambrian-age metamorphic rocks contain abundant mica schists and garnet-rich gneiss that weather to expose collectible specimens.

The Adirondacks were heavily glaciated during the Pleistocene, and post-glacial erosion continues to expose fresh mineral material along hillsides and in natural washes. Arnold Hill's moderate elevation and position in this metamorphic terrane make it a reliable spot for patient collectors willing to search outcrops and talus slopes. Garnet is the signature find here - look for deep red cubes and dodecahedrons in the weathered schist. Mica flakes can be found in large, shiny sheets, especially in quartz-rich layers. The site receives moderate foot traffic from local rockhounds and amateur geologists.

Best results come from working the upper slopes where bedrock is exposed and weathering has loosened specimens. This is a learn-as-you-go location - there are no formal trails or marked collecting areas, so success relies on scouting, patience, and basic geology knowledge. The surrounding forest provides shade and cover, making it pleasant to explore even in warmer months.
history: Arnold Hill lies within the Adirondack metamorphic complex, formed during the Grenville orogeny roughly 1 billion years ago when continental plates collided and deeply buried sedimentary and igneous rocks. The resulting metamorphism created garnet-bearing schists and mica-rich gneisses that today weather to produce the collectible specimens found here. Post-glacial landscape evolution in the last 10,000 years has continued to expose fresh material through erosion and mass wasting.
tips: Focus your search on exposed bedrock outcrops and talus slopes on the upper portions of the hill where weathering is most active; light-colored mica layers within darker schist indicate garnet-rich zones nearby. Bring a geological hammer or pry bar to gently extract specimens from weathered schist faces - avoid heavy excavation. Look for deep red or brownish cubic and dodecahedral garnet crystals, often found in glittery mica-rich bands; specimen size here ranges from pea-sized to marble-sized crystals, making it ideal for classroom collections and beginner geologists.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1223 feet. Wooded hillside with uneven, rocky terrain covered in leaf litter and moss. Steep slopes in places, with exposed bedrock and loose talus fragments common. No maintained trails - expect bushwhacking through mixed hardwood and conifer forest. Walking distance from parking is variable (typically 10-30 minutes), depending on which slope you choose to work. Footing is poor to moderate - sturdy hiking boots required. Some wet areas and small seasonal streams may be present.
best_season: May - October. Late spring through early fall offers the best combination of accessible terrain (snow melts by May in the northern Adirondacks), manageable temperatures, and visibility. Avoid November - April due to heavy snow, ice, and difficult access. Summer (June - August) can be warm but is reliable; early fall (September - October) is ideal with cooler temperatures and clear skies.
written_directions: From the Town of AuSable, take NY Route 9 north toward Champlain for approximately 2 miles. Turn right (east) onto Arnold Hill Road and continue 1.5 miles to a small parking area or pullout near the base of the hill. The exact approach varies depending on current private/public access agreements; confirm access locally before visiting. From Plattsburgh, take US Route 9 north 8 miles, then follow NY 9 south for 3 miles, then turn onto Arnold Hill Road.
road_conditions: Arnold Hill Road is an unpaved rural road, passable in a standard vehicle in dry conditions. May be rough or muddy after rain. Winter snow may close access.
parking_notes: Small informal pullout or roadside parking near the base of Arnold Hill Road. Capacity limited to 2-4 vehicles. No formal parking facilities. Confirm that parking is legal before leaving your vehicle.
collection_rules: Personal use rockhounding on public access areas only. Collect reasonable quantities of loose specimens and weathered material. Do not hammer or excavate bedrock extensively. Respect any posted private property signs. New York State does not require a permit for casual rockhounding on public land, but always verify access and ownership before collecting.
rules:
Personal use only
No commercial collection
Avoid excessive hammering of bedrock outcrops
Respect property boundaries and posted signs
Pack out trash
Leave gates as you find them
nearest_services: Champlain, NY (approximately 4 miles south) has gas stations, restaurants, and a general store. Plattsburgh, NY (approximately 10 miles south) offers more comprehensive services including larger grocers, emergency medical facilities, and rock shops.
accessibility_notes: Uneven, rocky terrain with steep slopes in places. No maintained trails or wheelchair access. Requires sturdy hiking boots and good balance. Bushwhacking through forest is necessary. Not suitable for young children or those with limited mobility. Expect a moderately strenuous hike with elevation gain. Bring adequate water and a headlamp if exploring late in the day.

faq.question: What gems can you find rockhounding at Arnold Hill near AuSable, New York?
faq.answer: Arnold Hill is known for almandine garnets (dark red to brown crystals), muscovite mica (large shiny sheets), and quartz specimens. Garnets are the signature find here, typically appearing as small cubes and dodecahedrons weathered from metamorphic schist. Collectors also encounter feldspar and other Adirondack metamorphic minerals.

faq.question: Is Arnold Hill free to rockhound, or do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Arnold Hill is free to access for casual rockhounding. No permit or fee is required for personal use collection on public land in New York. Always verify current access and property boundaries before visiting, as some portions may be private.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Arnold Hill?
faq.answer: Late May through early October is ideal. Summer offers reliable weather and clear visibility, while early fall (September-October) provides cooler temperatures. Avoid November through April due to heavy snow and ice in the northern Adirondacks, which makes terrain inaccessible and unsafe.

faq.question: Is Arnold Hill suitable for beginner rockhounds and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Arnold Hill is beginner-friendly with moderate difficulty. Children ages 8 and older can handle the terrain. The uneven, wooded hillside requires sturdy boots and attention to footing, but no special equipment or technical skills are needed. Families should go during daylight hours and bring water and a flashlight.

faq.question: What tools and techniques work best for collecting at Arnold Hill?
faq.answer: Bring a geological hammer, pry bar, and small chisel to gently extract specimens from weathered schist outcrops. Focus on areas where mica layers are visible and exposed bedrock is accessible. Avoid heavy excavation - most specimens can be found loose or require only light prying. A hand lens helps identify garnet crystals before removing them.

faq.question: How far is Arnold Hill from Plattsburgh, and what is the terrain like?
faq.answer: Arnold Hill is approximately 10 miles north of Plattsburgh near the Town of AuSable. Terrain is a wooded hillside at 1223 feet elevation with uneven, rocky footing, exposed bedrock outcrops, and loose talus. Expect steep slopes, leaf litter, and moss-covered rocks. No maintained trails exist, so bushwhacking is required. Sturdy hiking boots are essential.

meta_title: Arnold Hill Rockhounding - Find Garnet in New York
